---
title: "What is Riesz Regression?"
---

## The setup

In semiparametric inference, you often want to estimate a functional of an unknown nuisance:

$$
\theta(P) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[m(Z, g_0)\right]
$$

where $g_0(z) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z = z]$ is an outcome regression and $m(z, g)$ is a linear functional of $g$. Examples:

| Target | $m(z, g)$ |
|---|---|
| Average treatment effect | $g(1, x) - g(0, x)$ |
| Treatment-specific mean at $a^\star$ | $g(a^\star, x)$ |
| Additive shift effect $a \mapsto a + \delta$ | $g(a + \delta, x) - g(a, x)$ |
| Stochastic intervention $a \sim h(\cdot \mid x)$ | $\int g(a', x)\,h(a' \mid x)\,da'$ |

The plug-in estimator $\hat\theta = n^{-1} \sum_i m(Z_i, \hat g)$ is biased because $\hat g$ is itself estimated. Modern semiparametric estimators (one-step, TMLE, DML) correct that bias by adding a term involving the Riesz representer $\alpha_0$ of $m$:

$$
\hat\theta_{\text{corrected}} \;=\; n^{-1} \sum_i \Big[\,m(Z_i, \hat g) + \alpha_0(Z_i)\,\big(Y_i - \hat g(Z_i)\big)\,\Big].
$$

## What is $\alpha_0$?

By the Riesz representation theorem, since $m(z, \cdot)$ is bounded and linear in $g$, there exists a function $\alpha_0(z)$ satisfying

$$
\mathbb{E}[m(Z, g)] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha_0(Z) \cdot g(Z)\right]
\quad \text{for all } g.
$$

For ATE, $\alpha_0(a, x) = a/\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\pi(x))$ is the inverse-propensity weight. For TSM, $\alpha_0$ is the density ratio $\mathbb{1}\{a = a^\star\}/\pi(a^\star \mid x)$. In general, $\alpha_0$ has a closed form involving the generalized propensity score.

## Why not estimate the propensity directly?

For ATE you can: estimate $\hat\pi(x)$, plug in, get $\hat\alpha(a, x) = a/\hat\pi(x) - (1-a)/(1-\hat\pi(x))$.

This breaks down when:

1. **Positivity is borderline.** If $\hat\pi(x)$ approaches 0 or 1, $\hat\alpha$ explodes. Truncation patches the symptom but introduces its own bias.

2. **The estimand is non-standard.** For continuous shifts, stochastic interventions, and longitudinal LMTPs, the closed form involves derivatives of the conditional density of $A$ given $X$. Those are harder to estimate than $\alpha_0$ itself.

3. **Misspecification compounds.** Plug-in error in $\hat\pi$ propagates nonlinearly through $1/\hat\pi$.

## Riesz regression

Chernozhukov et al. ([2021](https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.14737)) observed that $\alpha_0$ is the unique minimizer of the Riesz loss

$$
L(\alpha) \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\alpha(Z)^2 - 2\,m(Z, \alpha)\right]
$$

over all square-integrable $\alpha$. The Gateaux derivative at $\alpha_0$ vanishes by the Riesz identity. Estimating $\alpha_0$ becomes a regression problem with a custom loss: no propensity, no density derivative, no truncation.

The minimizer is well-defined regardless of the closed-form tails. Where $\pi(x)$ is small, the squared-loss penalty shrinks $\hat\alpha$ toward zero in low-overlap regions, which controls downstream variance.

## Pick a fitter

Riesz regression with the squared loss is a standard regression problem. Different fitters trade off the same way they do for outcome regression:

- **[Gradient boosting](backends/boosting.qmd)** — the standard tool for tabular data. `rieszboost` plugs the per-row gradient and Hessian into xgboost's custom-objective interface. Hyperparameters that work for outcome models transfer.
- **[Kernel ridge regression](backends/kernel.qmd)** — closed form on small/medium data, exactly satisfies the variational characterization. `krrr` solves the linear system per λ; no boosting iterations.
- **[Random forests](backends/forest.qmd)** — non-parametric and well-suited to many covariates. `forestriesz` ships two flavors built on EconML's GRF: a moment-style fit with honest-split confidence intervals, and an augmentation-style fit that handles every estimand (including custom ones) without a sieve.

The three backends produce different $\hat\alpha$, but all are minimizers of the same population objective and converge to $\alpha_0$ under their respective consistency conditions.

## What `rieszreg` provides

A scikit-learn estimator (and an R6 mirror) that:

- Fits $\hat\alpha$ for `ATE`, `ATT`, `TSM`, `AdditiveShift`, `LocalShift`, `StochasticIntervention`, or [your own estimand](estimands.qmd).
- Composes with `cross_val_predict`, `GridSearchCV`, `Pipeline`.
- Surfaces near-positivity and outlier-extrapolation warnings via `.diagnose(X)`.

## Related work

A few existing tools cover overlapping ground.

[**genriesz**](https://github.com/MasaKat0/genriesz) (Kato, 2026) is a single Python package implementing the Bregman-unified Riesz regression framework from [arXiv:2601.07752](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.07752). It exposes `LinearFunctional` and `BregmanGenerator` abstractions, analogous to this project's `Estimand` and `LossSpec`. It ships several basis-function classes (polynomial, random Fourier features, Nyström, KNN catchments, random-forest leaves, PyTorch embeddings) inside the package itself. Third parties cannot publish their own learners against a stable protocol. It is Python only.

[**EconML**](https://github.com/py-why/EconML) (Microsoft) provides `RieszNet`, `ForestRiesz`, and an `automatic_debiased_ml` module. The `forestriesz` package in this family wraps EconML's `BaseGRF`. EconML is monolithic, with no third-party backend protocol, and Python only.

[**DoubleML**](https://docs.doubleml.org/) (Bach, Chernozhukov, Kurz, Spindler) is a mature DML library with parallel Python and R implementations. It expects the user to supply outcome and propensity nuisances using sklearn-compatible learners. Riesz regression is not the focal abstraction.

[**tlverse**](https://tlverse.org/) (van der Laan group) is an R-only family of packages (`sl3`, `tmle3`, `lmtp`, `hal9001`, …) organized around TMLE and SuperLearner. The meta-package + sibling-backends shape is the closest organizational match to this project.

What's distinctive here:

- The `Backend` / `MomentBackend` split, exposed as a stable Protocol, lets a third-party learner package depend on `rieszreg` and ship as its own PyPI/CRAN release. New learners do not require a PR upstream.
- The split itself reflects two structurally different fitting strategies: augmentation-style (kernel ridge, gradient boosting via `fit_augmented`) vs. moment-style (forests, neural nets via `fit_rows`).
- Cross-language Python + R coverage at the family level via R6 wrappers per package, not just bindings to a Python core.

Next: [install](install.qmd) and the [quickstart](index.qmd#quickstart) on the landing page.
